ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473); no specific entry for a customisable Singaporean deluxe noodle bowl exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers noodle-based dishes broadly. No specific code for a customisable multi-topping Singaporean noodle bowl exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for a customisable Singaporean noodle bowl dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a single bowl of approximately 450 g, comprising a base of egg noodles with assorted toppings including prawns, pork slices, fish balls, and blanched vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for comparable Singaporean noodle dishes (e.g., prawn noodle soup, wonton noodle soup) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (egg noodles, pork, prawns, fish balls). The per-100g composite reflects a weighted average of typical noodle bowl composition. Input values from the GrabFood listing (confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) were retained where consistent with HPB and USDA reference ranges and adjusted where discrepancies exceeded 10%. The GrabFood listing itself is not used as a primary nutrient source; it served only as the initial record trigger. All nutrient values are anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary source).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a customisable Singaporean deluxe noodle bowl. GI of 52 is estimated using cooked egg noodles as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, which has a published GI of approximately 46–57 (mean ~52) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Both are approved GI sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (52 × 54.9) / 100 ≈ 28.6, where available carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 12.2 × 4.5 = 54.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce, limit or omit fish balls (processed seafood with high intrinsic sodium), and avoid adding chilli sauce, as the combined sodium load from a standard serving is clinically significant and may exceed safe single-meal thresholds.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 12.2 g/100 g from refined egg noodles; a 450 g serving provides approximately 54.9 g available carbohydrate with an estimated glycemic load of 28.6 (high GL category).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives, control portion size to reduce glycemic load, and rely on the protein content (approximately 35 g per serving from prawns, pork, and fish balls) to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.6 g/100 g and cholesterol at 33.3 mg/100 g; sodium at 400 mg/100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request lean protein substitutions (e.g., replace pork slices with additional prawns or tofu), omit fried shallots to reduce saturated fat intake, and choose low-sodium soy sauce to manage the combined saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork slices contribute arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to RA; processed fish balls add high sodium worsening tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request a pork-free version substituting additional vegetables and prawns, omit fish balls to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling, and monitor individual response to chilli sauce as a nightshade-derived condiment.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Deluxe Noodle Bowl is a customisable Singaporean noodle dish served in a single bowl of approximately 450 g. It features a choice of premium noodles — typically egg noodles — as the carbohydrate base, accompanied by up to six complimentary toppings that commonly include prawns, pork slices, fish balls, blanched choy sum (Chinese flowering cabbage), fried shallots, and soy-based sauce, with optional additions such as chilli sauce. The noodles are boiled and blanched before assembly, yielding a tender, slightly chewy texture. The broth or sauce is savoury and umami-forward, with the fried shallots contributing a mild sweetness and crunch. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 122 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 7.8 g/100 g from the mixed animal proteins (prawns, pork, fish balls), moderate carbohydrate at 12.2 g/100 g, and fat at 4.4 g/100 g. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg/100 g, making a full 450 g serving a high-sodium meal (~1800 mg) that warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 52 (low-GI range) and glycemic load of approximately 28.6 per serving reflect the moderate glycaemic impact of egg noodles, partially offset by the protein content. The dish is not suitable for halal diets without modification due to pork content, and contains common allergens including gluten, egg, shellfish, fish, and soy. It is a popular hawker-style and food-delivery meal in Singapore, reflecting the Chinese-Singaporean culinary tradition of mixed noodle bowls.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a deluxe noodle bowl?
Is noodle bowl healthy for diabetics?
Sodium content of Singapore noodle bowl
Glycemic index of egg noodles Singapore
Can I eat noodle bowl with high blood pressure?
What allergens are in a noodle bowl?
Deluxe noodle bowl calories per serving
Is noodle bowl suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values originate from a GrabFood listing with ai_generated provenance and confidence 0.7. Values have been cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for comparable Singaporean noodle dishes and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. The dish is customisable with variable toppings, introducing inherent compositional variability. Medium confidence is appropriate as no single approved-source entry for this exact dish exists; values represent a defensible adapted estimate anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this customisable multi-topping Singaporean noodle bowl are not individually reported as a composite dish in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. These fields cannot be reliably derived without a full laboratory analysis of the assembled dish. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g/100 g per the input source; this low value is plausible for a dish containing pork and processed fish balls and is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar components. The dish is highly customisable; actual nutrient values will vary depending on noodle type selected and toppings chosen. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on cooked egg noodles per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The nutrient_source URL has been updated to the official HPB Singapore food and beverage nutrient composition page (https://www.hpb.gov.sg/food-beverage/food-beverage-nutrient-composition) to ensure approved-source compliance; the GrabFood listing is not used as a primary nutrient source. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Deluxe Noodle Bowl', a precise canonical English name. All mandatory Singaporean local-language names are present: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (122.0), protein_g (7.8), fat_g (4.4), carbohydrate_g (12.2) are all present, numeric, and non-zero. nutrient_source now cites HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary source) with the official HPB URL. GrabFood listing is explicitly excluded as a primary nutrient source. Source match type is 'adapted' with full explanation.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.9), sodium_mg (400.0), saturated_fat_g (1.6), and sugar_g (1.1) are all present as numeric values, plausible, and supported.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (52), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (28.6), and gi_source are all present. GI source cites University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), both approved GI sources. Analogous estimate basis is fully documented. gi_source URL points to glycemicindex.com (University of Sydney GI Database).

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 ('noodle dish') and FoodEx2 A0FJP ('noodle-based dish') are defensible anchors. Match types corrected to 'closest_parent' per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters), covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key 'type2_diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid Arthritis is included as an additional condition.
